title: Generation criteria for the Fukaya category
speaker: Mohammed Abouzaid

proof_stages.label: Kontsevich skeleton-cosheaf target
proof_stages.summary: The talk is framed by Kontsevich's conjecture that a Stein/Liouville skeleton should carry a cosheaf of A-infinity algebras or categories whose module category encodes compact Fukaya objects.

proof_stages.label: Constructible families of Lagrangians over a simplicial complex
proof_stages.summary: The skeleton is replaced by an abstract simplicial complex Q carrying smooth exact Lagrangian families on each simplex, with face inclusions and a local-constancy condition on Legendrian boundaries so that Floer continuation remains valid.

proof_stages.label: Cosheaf of local Fukaya categories
proof_stages.summary: For each simplex sigma, the category B_sigma has as objects the components of the Lagrangian family at the barycenter and Floer morphism spaces, while continuation along face paths gives A-infinity corestriction functors that become strictly cosheaf-like after subdivision.

proof_stages.label: Module cosheaf associated to a compact Lagrangian
proof_stages.summary: A compact exact Lagrangian K defines compatible left modules HF^*(K,L_sigma) over the local categories, and similarly right modules HF^*(L_sigma,K), with compatibility encoded by higher A-infinity homotopies.

proof_stages.label: Hochschild cosheaf and family open-closed map
proof_stages.summary: The Hochschild homologies of the local categories, described via cyclic bar complexes and holomorphic disks, assemble into a cosheaf whose homology maps by a global open-closed map to H_*(X, boundary X).

proof_stages.label: Fundamental-class generation criterion
proof_stages.summary: The sufficient generation criterion is that the fundamental class of X lies in the image of the global open-closed map, in which case HF^*(K,K) is recovered as the derived tensor product or Tor of the left and right module cosheaves.

proof_stages.label: Commutative diagram and annulus degeneration
proof_stages.summary: A commutative diagram involving the open-closed map, Poincare duality, and holomorphic-disk maps through the Tor bar complex shows that a chosen preimage of the fundamental class acts as the identity, with the key compatibility proved by rotating, gluing, and degenerating holomorphic disks to an annulus.

examples.name: Cotangent bundle of a closed manifold
examples.note: The exact cotangent bundle T^*Q is the known case where the cosheaf is constant and the compact Fukaya category embeds into local systems on Q, serving as a concrete benchmark for the conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites the cotangent bundle of a closed manifold as the one understood case and states the constant-cosheaf/local-systems description without doing a computation on it.

passes.reason: standard infinite-family cotangent bundle used as the known positive case.
A cotangent bundle T*Q of an arbitrary closed manifold is a standard systematic Liouville example used as the known case of the conjecture.
an arbitrary cotangent bundle T*Q is the standard understood cotangent-bundle case with constant cosheaf/local systems

A standard systematic cotangent-bundle case used as the known positive theorem.
A cotangent bundle of an arbitrary closed manifold is the standard smooth-skeleton case used as a positive theorem, not a failure case.
A cotangent bundle of an arbitrary closed manifold is used as the standard understood case of the conjecture, not as a failure case, and is a systematic family rather than structurally special.

Used as the standard understood positive case of the conjecture, and a cotangent bundle of a closed manifold is a broad ordinary family rather than a sporadic, extremal, degenerate, or maximally symmetric object.
standard systematic cotangent-bundle case where the conjectural sheaf is understood
used as the standard understood positive case of the conjecture, in an infinite family of cotangent bundles
It is used as the standard understood theorem case, and an arbitrary cotangent bundle of a closed manifold is a systematic non-sporadic model.
standard understood theorem case of a cotangent bundle, a systematic family with no sporadic or limiting structure

examples.name: Pair of pants with trivalent skeleton
examples.note: The one-dimensional pair-of-pants picture, with A_2 categories at trivalent vertices, is used to reveal the central idea of assigning local Lagrangian families to cells and later to visualize the open-closed image filling the fundamental class.
examples.engagement_reason: He repeatedly works through the pair of pants picture, identifying A2 pieces, Lagrangians and cones, and later analyzing Hochschild/open-closed contributions from edges and the trivalent vertex via holomorphic triangles.

passes.reason: basic pair-of-pants test case used to illustrate the cosheaf and open-closed construction.
The pair of pants with its three-legged trivalent skeleton is used as a regular symmetric model for the local cosheaf picture, not to refute a claim.
the standard pair of pants with its trivalent skeleton is used as a symmetric regular model for the cosheaf construction
the pair of pants with a trivalent skeleton is a standard local model used constructively rather than as a failure case.
The pair of pants with its trivalent skeleton is a basic illustrative Liouville example, not a sporadic, collapsed, or adversarial case.
The pair of pants with a trivalent skeleton is a basic illustrative Liouville-surface example for the cosheaf construction, not a sporadic or adversarial object.
The pair of pants with its trivalent skeleton is used as a motivating model for the cosheaf construction, not to refute a claim, and it is a standard non-sporadic Liouville-surface example.

Used as an illustrative positive model for the cosheaf construction with a trivalent skeleton, not to refute a claim, and the pair of pants is a standard systematic building block rather than a strict structural exception.

used as a positive motivating model, and it is the minimal/basic non-smooth skeleton case in dimension one

examples.name: Boundary-collision family over an edge
examples.note: A family whose Legendrian boundary intersections appear or disappear along an edge illustrates the forbidden non-constructible behavior and motivates the local-constancy condition needed for continuation maps.
examples.engagement_reason: He draws and analyzes the family over an edge where boundary intersections collide, using it to check and explain failure of the local-constancy condition.

passes.reason: collapsed boundary-intersection family violates local constancy and is presented as the phenomenon to avoid.

the family reaches a boundary-collision limiting stratum where Legendrian boundary intersections collapse, and is used as the bad case excluded by local constancy.
A limiting boundary-collision family where Legendrian boundary points coalesce, introduced as the forbidden non-locally-constant phenomenon.
The family has a collapsed boundary collision and is introduced as the phenomenon local constancy is meant to exclude.
The family in which boundary points collide over an edge is explicitly introduced as the bad phenomenon excluded by local constancy, and the collision collapses distinct boundary data.
Presented as the phenomenon to be avoided because local constancy fails when boundary intersections collide, with the collision giving a collapsed or degenerate family.

examples.name: Single-point parameter space that cannot see K
examples.note: Taking Q to be a point parametrizing only one noncompact Lagrangian shows that HF^*(K,K) cannot be recovered in complete generality, prompting the Hochschild/open-closed generation criterion.
examples.engagement_reason: He constructs the one-point parameter example with a chosen Lagrangian and K and reasons through why the resulting data cannot recover Floer cohomology of K.

passes.reason: minimal one-point parameter space misses K and shows recovery is impossible in full generality.
A one-point parameter space is the minimal degenerate simplicial complex and is used to show that the general setup cannot recover HF(K,K).
the one-point parameter space is trivial and is used to show the general setup cannot recover HF(K,K) without a generation condition
a one-point parameter space is the minimal trivial parameter complex and is used to show that the general setup cannot recover HF(K,K).
A trivial one-point parameter complex is used to show that the unqualified recovery of HF(K,K) can fail.
The one-point parameter space is the minimal simplicial base and is used to show that the general setup cannot recover HF(K,K).
The one-point parameter space is used to show that the general setup cannot recover HF(K,K), and as a singleton it is a trivial minimal parameter space.
Used to show that the general setup cannot recover HF(K,K), and the one-point parameter space is a minimal trivial parameter complex.
trivial one-point parameter space is used to show the general setup cannot recover Floer cohomology
the one-point parameter space is a trivial/minimal case used to show the general setup cannot recover HF(K,K)
